message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="kent-beck" color="yellow" summary="Task 4 prep, registration form accessibility">
Heydon, Kent here. The foundation work is done (scaffolding, walking skeleton, design tokens). The mob is moving to Task 4: User Registration -- our first real HTML form.

I want to make sure accessibility is baked in from the very first test, not bolted on after. Here's what I'm thinking about for the registration form:

1. **Visible `<label>` elements** for email and password fields -- our agreements explicitly prohibit placeholder-only inputs.
2. **Validation error association**: `aria-describedby` linking error messages to their inputs.
3. **Live regions**: `aria-live="polite"` for validation errors so screen readers announce them.
4. **Semantic HTML**: `<form>`, `<fieldset>`, `<legend>` if appropriate, proper `<button type="submit">` with a descriptive label ("Create account", not "Submit").
5. **Focus management**: After form submission errors, focus should move to the first field with an error, or to an error summary.
6. **Page structure**: Skip link, `<main>` landmark, proper heading hierarchy.

When Luca starts writing the Askama template for the registration page, I'd like you reviewing it in real time. Can you outline the semantic HTML structure you'd want for a registration form? That gives us a target to write tests against -- we can assert on roles, labels, and ARIA attributes in our integration tests.

What accessibility patterns should we test for in the registration form?
</teammate-message>

content.text: Kent, this is exactly the right question at exactly the right time. Let me give you the complete semantic HTML structure and the testable assertions.

input.content:
Kent, brilliant. Writing accessibility assertions into the first test means every subsequent form inherits the pattern. Here's the complete specification. **The semantic HTML structure for the registration page:** ```html Create account - Todo List Skip to main content

Todo List

Create account

{% if errors.email %}{{ errors.email }}{% endif %}

At least 8 characters

{% if errors.password %}{{ errors.password }}{% endif %}

{% if errors.password_confirm %}{{ errors.password_confirm }}{% endif %}

Already have an account? Sign in

© 2026 Todo List

``` **Design decisions and why:** 1. **No `
` / ``** -- for a simple 3-field form, fieldset adds verbosity to screen reader announcements without adding clarity. Fieldset is valuable when you have groups of related inputs (e.g., "Shipping address" vs "Billing address"). A single registration form doesn't need it. 2. **`novalidate` on the form** -- we handle validation server-side. Browser-native validation messages are inconsistent across browsers and poorly localizable. Our server-side errors are associated via `aria-describedby` and announced via `role="alert"`. 3. **`aria-describedby` with multiple IDs on the password field** -- `aria-describedby="password-hint password-error"` links the input to both the hint ("At least 8 characters") and any error. Screen readers concatenate these: "Password, text input, At least 8 characters, Password is too short." Both pieces of information in one announcement. 4. **Error elements always in the DOM** -- the `

` is always present even when empty. This ensures the `aria-describedby` reference is always valid. When content appears, `role="alert"` causes immediate announcement. 5. **`value="{{ form_data.email }}"` on email but NOT on password fields** -- on validation failure, we re-populate the email so the user doesn't retype it. We never re-populate passwords (security). 6. **`autocomplete` attributes** -- `email` for email, `new-password` for both password fields. Password managers use these to offer the right suggestions. Screen readers use them to identify field purpose. This is also WCAG 1.3.5 (Identify Input Purpose). 7. **Status announcer outside `

`** -- it's never inside an HTMX swap target. It persists across all interactions. **Testable accessibility assertions for integration tests:** ```rust // Page structure tests fn registration_page_has_lang_attribute() fn registration_page_has_descriptive_title() // contains "Create account" fn registration_page_has_skip_link_targeting_main() fn registration_page_has_main_landmark() fn registration_page_has_exactly_one_h1() fn registration_page_h1_matches_page_purpose() // "Create account" // Form structure tests fn registration_form_has_email_input_with_label() fn registration_form_has_password_input_with_label() fn registration_form_has_password_confirm_input_with_label() fn registration_form_submit_button_has_descriptive_text() // "Create account" not "Submit" fn registration_form_email_has_autocomplete_email() fn registration_form_passwords_have_autocomplete_new_password() // Label association tests fn registration_form_every_input_has_associated_label() // for/id match fn registration_form_every_input_has_aria_describedby() // Error handling tests fn registration_with_invalid_email_shows_error_linked_to_field() fn registration_with_short_password_shows_error_linked_to_field() fn registration_with_mismatched_passwords_shows_error_linked_to_field() fn registration_errors_have_role_alert() fn registration_with_errors_repopulates_email_field() fn registration_with_errors_does_not_repopulate_password_fields() // Security tests (also accessibility-relevant) fn registration_with_duplicate_email_shows_generic_error() // no account enumeration // Navigation tests fn registration_page_has_link_to_login() ``` **How to assert these in Rust integration tests:** Parse the response body HTML and check for: - `html[lang="en"]` exists - `title` contains expected text - `a.skip-link[href="#main-content"]` exists - `main#main-content` exists - Exactly one `h1` element - For each input: a `label[for]` matching the input's `id` - For each input: `aria-describedby` attribute present and referencing existing element IDs - Error elements have `role="alert"` - `button[type="submit"]` text content is "Create account" I'd recommend adding `scraper` crate (HTML parser) as a dev dependency for these assertions. It lets you use CSS selectors on the response HTML, making the test assertions read naturally. **The smallest first test (red step):** ```rust #[tokio::test] async fn registration_page_returns_200_with_form() { let app = spawn_app().await; let response = app.get("/register").await; assert_eq!(response.status(), 200); let html = response.text().await.unwrap(); let doc = scraper::Html::parse_document(&html); // Page has correct structure assert!(doc.select(&sel("html[lang='en']")).next().is_some()); assert!(doc.select(&sel("main#main-content")).next().is_some()); assert!(doc.select(&sel("a.skip-link[href='#main-content']")).next().is_some()); assert_eq!(doc.select(&sel("h1")).count(), 1); // Form has labeled inputs assert!(doc.select(&sel("label[for='email']")).next().is_some()); assert!(doc.select(&sel("input#email[type='email']")).next().is_some()); assert!(doc.select(&sel("label[for='password']")).next().is_some()); assert!(doc.select(&sel("input#password[type='password']")).next().is_some()); // Submit button has descriptive text let button = doc.select(&sel("button[type='submit']")).next().unwrap(); assert!(button.text().collect::().contains("Create account")); } ``` This single test validates the entire accessible structure. Write it first (red), then build the template to make it pass (green). The template structure I gave above is the target. I'll be reviewing every template Luca creates in real time. Let's build this right from the first line.
input.summary: Complete registration form HTML spec and test assertions

routing.summary: Complete registration form HTML spec and test assertions

Write it first (red), then build the template to make it pass (green). The template structure I gave above is the target. I'll be reviewing every template Luca creates in real time. Let's build this right from the first line.

content.text:
That's my most important contribution so far -- a complete, testable HTML specification for the registration form. Kent now has concrete test assertions to write first (red), and whoever drives the template implementation has a clear target to make those tests pass (green).

Let me also send this to Luca since he'll be the one implementing it.

input.content:
Luca, Kent and I are preparing for Task #4 (registration). I've sent Kent the complete semantic HTML specification for the registration form and a set of testable accessibility assertions. Key things you'll need when building the Askama template: 1. **Base template** (if not already created) needs: ``, skip link with `.skip-link` class, `
`, status announcer div, viewport meta WITHOUT zoom restrictions. 2. **Registration template** extends base and contains: - `Create account - Todo List` (dynamic, descriptive) - `

Create account

` (one h1 per page) - Form with `method="post" action="/register" novalidate` - Each field: `
input.summary: Registration form template spec for Task 4

Luca, Kent and I are preparing for Task #4 (registration). I've sent Kent the complete semantic HTML specification for the registration form and a set of testable accessibility assertions. Key things you'll need when building the Askama template: 1. **Base template** (if not already created) needs: ``, skip link with `.skip-link` class, `
`, status announcer div, viewport meta WITHOUT zoom restrictions. 2. **Registration template** extends base and contains: - `Create account - Todo List` (dynamic, descriptive) - `

content.text: Good. The registration form accessibility specification is now shared with both Kent (who will write the tests) and Luca (who will build the template). The mob has a clear, testable target for the first real HTML form in the application. Accessibility assertions are embedded in the integration tests from the very first feature -- not as a separate concern, but as part of what it means for the form to work correctly.
